title: Server Room Commissioning
description: Put the data center server room commissioning steps in the correct order, from construction completion to live operations.

steps.text: Perform deep clean of room — remove construction debris and dust
Install raised floor system with proper pedestal heights and leveling
Install and start CRAH cooling units, bring room to operating temperature
Power on UPS system and verify battery charge and bypass operation
Cool room to target temperature (68-72°F) and stabilize humidity (40-60% RH)
Perform full load bank test on UPS and generator transfer
Install servers and IT equipment into racks
Verify environmental monitoring, alarms, and DCIM integration

explanation: Server room commissioning follows a strict sequence. The room must be clean before floor installation to prevent particulate contamination. Cooling must be operational before IT equipment is installed — servers powered on in an uncooled room can overheat in minutes. Load bank testing verifies power infrastructure under controlled conditions before risking live equipment.

title: UPS Battery Replacement
description: Sequence the steps for safely replacing UPS battery strings in a live data center without interrupting IT load.

steps.text: Notify operations and obtain change management approval
Transfer UPS to internal maintenance bypass mode
Verify load is being carried by the bypass path using output metering
Open battery disconnect breaker and verify zero voltage at terminals
Remove old battery strings, following proper PPE and handling procedures
Install new battery strings and torque all connections to specification
Close battery disconnect breaker and verify charging current
Return UPS to normal online (double-conversion) mode and verify operation

explanation: Battery replacement in a live data center requires careful coordination. The UPS bypass ensures load continuity during the maintenance window. Verifying zero voltage before touching batteries prevents DC arc flash incidents. New batteries must charge fully before the UPS is returned to normal mode to ensure backup runtime.

title: Hot Aisle Containment Installation
description: Order the steps for retrofitting hot aisle containment on an existing server rack row.

steps.text: Position racks in proper hot/cold aisle alignment with consistent row spacing
Install blanking panels in all empty U-spaces within racks
Mount end wall frames and panels at both ends of the hot aisle
Install containment roof panel framework spanning between rack tops
Place roof panels and seal gaps between racks and roof structure
Install self-closing doors with magnetic latches at end walls
Verify containment integrity and seal any remaining air gaps

explanation: Hot aisle containment must be built from the rack outward. Blanking panels seal the rack-level bypasses first, then end walls and roof panels create the enclosure. Doors go last since they need the frame structure in place. Sealing gaps is the final critical step — even small leaks significantly reduce containment effectiveness.

title: Clean Agent Suppression Activation Sequence
description: Order the events that occur during an automatic clean agent fire suppression activation, from initial detection to agent hold time.

steps.text: VESDA air sampling detects smoke at Alert threshold (earliest warning)
VESDA reaches Action threshold — first alarm stage activates
Second detector confirms fire condition (cross-zone verification)
Pre-discharge alarm activates — audible horn and visible strobes
Countdown timer begins (typically 30-60 seconds) for personnel evacuation
Abort window — manual abort switch can halt discharge if false alarm
Solenoid valve opens and clean agent discharges into protected space
Room pressurizes and agent must maintain design concentration for required hold time (10+ minutes)

explanation: Clean agent suppression follows a precise sequence designed to balance rapid fire response with personnel safety. Cross-zone verification prevents false discharges. The pre-discharge alarm and abort window give occupants time to evacuate. The hold time ensures the fire is fully extinguished and cannot reignite. Per NFPA 2001, every step is mandatory.

title: Generator Load Bank Test
description: Sequence the steps for performing a full load bank test on a standby diesel generator.

steps.text: Inspect generator — check oil level, coolant, belts, and battery charger
Verify fuel day tank level and fuel supply system operation
Start generator at no-load and verify stable voltage and frequency
Apply 25% load from load bank and monitor for 30 minutes
Increase to 50% rated load and monitor temperatures and pressures
Increase to 75% rated load and verify all parameters within limits
Increase to 100% rated load and run for minimum 2 hours per NFPA 110
Remove load incrementally, run at no-load for cool-down, then shutdown

explanation: Load bank testing proves the generator can deliver its rated capacity under controlled conditions. NFPA 110 requires annual load testing. Gradual load stepping prevents thermal shock to the engine. The 100% load run for 2 hours proves sustained capacity. Cool-down at no-load allows turbocharger bearings to cool before oil flow stops.

title: Raised Floor Installation
description: Order the steps for installing a raised floor system in a new data center room.

steps.text: Establish reference grid layout and mark pedestal locations on slab
Set pedestal heights using laser level to achieve uniform floor plane
Level and secure pedestals to floor slab with adhesive or mechanical fasteners
Install stringers connecting pedestals to create structural grid
Lay standard solid floor tiles starting from reference corner
Cut and fit perimeter tiles at walls and around columns
Install perforated tiles at planned CRAH discharge locations and cold aisles

explanation: Raised floor installation requires precision leveling to support equipment loads and maintain uniform plenum depth for airflow. Pedestals must be secured and leveled before tiles go down. Perforated tiles go last because their placement depends on the final CRAH and rack layout. Floor flatness is typically specified at +/- 1mm per TIA-942.

title: PDU Energization Sequence
description: Sequence the steps for safely energizing a new floor-mount PDU in a live data center.

steps.text: Verify all lockout/tagout devices are removed and clearance is granted
Close utility main breaker and verify incoming voltage at switchgear
Verify ATS (automatic transfer switch) is in normal source position
Start UPS and verify it enters online double-conversion mode
Verify UPS battery bank is charging and at acceptable voltage
Close PDU input breaker and verify transformer output voltage
Energize PDU output bus and verify voltage at panelboard
Close individual branch circuit breakers and verify output at rack PDU strips

explanation: PDU energization follows a strict top-down sequence from source to load. Each step is verified before proceeding to the next to catch wiring errors, transformer issues, or protection coordination problems before they affect downstream equipment. Energizing out of sequence can damage equipment or cause arc flash incidents.

title: CRAH Chilled Water Startup
description: Order the steps for starting up a CRAH (Computer Room Air Handler) unit connected to a chilled water plant.

steps.text: Verify chilled water isolation valves are open on supply and return
Open chilled water supply valve slowly to prevent water hammer
Vent trapped air at high-point air vents on the coil piping
Inspect all connections and fittings for leaks under pressure
Start chilled water pump and verify design flow rate at balancing valve
Enable CRAH cooling mode via the unit controller
Verify chilled water supply temperature at coil inlet (typically 42-45°F)
Check supply air temperature downstream of CRAH and adjust setpoints

explanation: CRAH chilled water startup must be methodical to avoid water hammer, air locks, and leaks. Opening valves slowly prevents pressure surges. Venting air is critical — trapped air blocks flow through the coil and reduces cooling capacity. Verifying flow rate and temperatures confirms the unit can meet its design cooling load.
